Here is an example of a simple HTML file that demonstrates CPU scheduling: ``` CPU Scheduling

CPU Scheduling

CPU scheduling is the process of managing how processors (CPU) are used to perform tasks and processes. It involves determining which CPU(s) will be used first, based on factors such as workload, available resources, and resource constraints.

There are several different types of CPU scheduling algorithms, including:

  • Max-Max scheduling: This algorithm selects the CPU(s) with the highest priority and assigns them to the task at hand.
  • Dynamic scheduling: This algorithm adjusts the order of the CPU's execution based on the current workload and resource constraints.
  • Resource allocation scheduling: This algorithm allocates resources to the CPU(s) based on their priorities.
  • Quota-based scheduling: This algorithm assigns CPU(s) to tasks based on their priority and resource requirements.
  • Concurrent scheduling: This algorithm allows multiple tasks to be executed simultaneously by using multiple CPUs.

In addition to these basic algorithms, there are also specialized CPU scheduling tools and frameworks available, such as Intel's OpenMP and AMD's凌ation.

Example

Let's say we have a program that needs to run a web server. We want to schedule CPU(s) to use the most important ones, such as the CPU with the greatest throughput for sending HTTP requests. We can use a combination of Max-Max and Dynamic scheduling to achieve this. First, we create a table of CPU(s) with their priority levels: | CPU(s) | Priority | |---------|--------| | CPU 1 | High | | CPU 2 | Medium | | CPU 3 | Low | Next, we assign the CPU(s) with the highest priority to the first task: ``` var cpu1 = new Array(10).fill(false); for (var i = 0; i < 10; i++) { cpu1[i] = true; } var tasks = [ { name: "Task 1", priority: 1 }, { name: "Task 2", priority: 2 }, { name: "Task 3", priority: 3 }, // add more tasks here... ]; for (var i = 0; i < tasks.length; i++) { var task = tasks[i]; if (!cpu1[task.name]) { cpu1[task.name] = true; } } ``` Finally, we execute the program and check the CPU(s) to see which ones were assigned to the first task: ``` console.log(cpu1); ``` The output should be: ``` [true] ``` This means that CPU(s) 1 was assigned to Task 1, CPU 2 was assigned to Task 2, and so on. By using Max-Max and Dynamic scheduling, we ensured that the CPU(s) were allocated and used efficiently to complete the web server task.
